elements.sent-2411-001: We study convex optimization problems where disjoint blocks of variables are controlled by binary indicator variables that are also subject to conditions, e.g., cardinality.
elements.sent-2411-002: Several classes of important examples can be formulated in such a way that both the objective and the constraints are separable convex quadratics.
elements.sent-2411-003: We describe a family of polynomial-time approximation algorithms and negative complexity results.
elements.sent-2411-004: We consider optimization problems on $n$ real variables $x$ that are partitioned into a set $B$ of blocks; the $i^{th}$ block is denoted $B_i$ and we write $x{[i]}$ for the subvector of $x$ restricted to $B_i$.
elements.sent-2411-005: Corresponding to each block there is a binary variable $z_i$.
elements.sent-2411-006: The problems have the following structure (additional notation below): \begin{subequations \begin{equation}[QIB]: \ \min \ \sum_{i = 1}^{|B|} x[i]^T Q^0_i x[i] \ + \ \sum_{j = 1}^n d^0_j x^2_j \ + \ \sum_{j = 1}^n c^0_j x \ + \ \sum_{i = 1}^{|B|} v^0_i z_i s.t. \quad \sum_{i = 1}^{|B|} x[i]^T Q^r_i x[i] \ + \ \sum_{j = 1}^n d^r_j x^2_j \ + \ \sum_{j = 1}^n c^r_j x_j \ + \ \sum_{i = 1}^{|B|} v^r_i z_i \ \le \ p_r, \quad 1 \le r \le m \label{eq:mainbody} \ell_j \ \le \ x_j \le u_j, \ for \ 1 \le j \le n, \quad z_i \in \{0,1\} \ for \ 1 \le i \le |B|, for $1 \le i \le |B|$, if $z_i = 0$ then $x_j = 0$ for all $j \in B_i$, \label{eq:block}\end{equation} \end{subequations We assume that every inequality \eqref{eq:mainbody falls into one of two types:.}}}
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}[QIB]: \ \min \ \sum_{i = 1}^{|B|} x[i]^T Q^0_i x[i] \ + \ \sum_{j = 1}^n d^0_j x^2_j \ + \ \sum_{j = 1}^n c^0_j x \ + \ \sum_{i = 1}^{|B|} v^0_i z_i s.t. \quad \sum_{i = 1}^{|B|} x[i]^T Q^r_i x[i] \ + \ \sum_{j = 1}^n d^r_j x^2_j \ + \ \sum_{j = 1}^n c^r_j x_j \ + \ \sum_{i = 1}^{|B|} v^r_i z_i \ \le \ p_r, \quad 1 \le r \le m \label{eq:mainbody} \ell_j \ \le \ x_j \le u_j, \ for \ 1 \le j \le n, \quad z_i \in \{0,1\} \ for \ 1 \le i \le |B|, for $1 \le i \le |B|$, if $z_i = 0$ then $x_j = 0$ for all $j \in B_i$, \label{eq:block}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-007:
Mixed-integer constraints; those with $Q^r_i 
eq 0$ or $d^r
eq 0$ or $c^r
eq 0$.
elements.sent-2411-009: Here $Q^r_i = 0$ for all $i$ and $d^r_j = c^r_j = 0$ for all $j$, and $v^r_i \in \{0,1,-1\} \, \forall i$.
elements.sent-2411-010: Constraints \eqref{eq:block enforce the indicator relationship over blocks of variables.}
elements.sent-2411-011: Motivation for this type of model is given below; here we point out that combinatorial constraints include cardinality constraints as a special case; there is an abundant literature for this particular feature where the blocks are singletons, in which case problem \eqref{QuadBlockInd is (strongly) NP-hard\footnote{Our results extend to the simple generalization of combinatorial constraints where the $v^r_i$ can take integral values in some fixed range.. .}}
elements.sent-2411-012: In this paper we study the case where constraints \eqref{eq:mainbody exhibit a (new) parameterized structured sparsity condition that arises in a number of important problems, when appropriately reformulated.}
elements.sent-2411-013: We present an algorithm that under such conditions runs in polynomial time and yields solutions with provable approximation guarantees.
elements.sent-2411-014: We posit that this theoretical development helps explain why problems of the above form, while NP-hard, can (empirically) be effectively approximated.
elements.sent-2411-015: As a key ingredient in our approach, we introduce a representation of the sparsity structure of a problem of the above form: \begin{DE {\bf Constraint-block incidence.}}
elements.sent-2411-016: Given a problem of type QIB, we say that a constraint $r$ and a block $B_i$ are incident with each other if $Q^r_i \neq 0$, or $\sum_{j \in B_i (d^r_j + | c^r_j|) > 0$ or $| v^r_i | > 0$, in other words, if constraint $r$ includes at least one term related to block $B_i$.}
elements.sent-2411-017: The set of blocks incident with a given constraint $r$ is denoted by $\bB^r$.
elements.sent-2411-018: Given a problem of type QIB, its constraint-block intersection graph is the graph whose vertex-set is the set of constraints \eqref{eq:mainbody and there is an edge between two constraints, $r$ and $r'$, if there is a block $B_i$ that both $r$ and $r'$ are incident with.}
elements.sent-2411-020: For simplicity of language, we assume: \begin{AS (i) For $1 \le j \le n$, $\ell_j = 0$ and $u_j = 1$.}
elements.sent-2411-021: Let $\kappa$ denote the largest support of any combinatorial constraint, and let $m_c$ denote the number of combinatorial constraints.
elements.sent-2411-022: There is an algorithm that runs in time $O( (n + m)(2/\epsilon)^{2\omega + 2 \kappa^{2 \min\{ m_c, \omega + 1 \} )$, plus the time needed to solve $O(2|B|/\epsilon)$ convex QCQPs, each a restriction of \eqref{QuadBlockInd to a single block (hence with $\le \omega + 1$ rows) with appropriate right-hand side vectors; the algorithm either proves infeasibility or computes a vector that is (a) superoptimal, (b) feasible for the combinatorial constraints and (c) has maximum infeasibility $\le \max_r |\bB^r| \epsilon$ for the mixed-integer constraints.}}}
elements.sent-2411-023: In particular, \cite{BienstockMunoz2018 studies mixed-integer polynomial optimization problems under a different treewidth setup.}
elements.sent-2411-024: Problems of type QIB can have arbitrarily large blocks, and, as a result, the algorithm in \cite{BienstockMunoz2018 may have exponential runtime.}
elements.sent-2411-025: Also, there is an even larger literature on purely combinatorial problems under constant treewidth (see, e.g., \cite{bodlaender ).}
elements.sent-2411-026: In this context, one can use problems of type QIB, without mixed-integer constraints, to encode combinatorial problems.
elements.sent-2411-027: For example, Theorem \ref{thm:mainone states that we can solve, in polynomial time, a vertex packing problem on a graph whose line graph has bounded treewidth.}
elements.sent-2411-028: This paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-029: Section \ref{sec:twdef introduces tree-decompositions and related concepts.}
elements.sent-2411-030: Section \ref{sec:appl describes the application of our results to specific examples of problem QIB.}
elements.sent-2411-032: Section \ref{sec:nphard shows that even highly restricted versions of problem QIB are already NP-hard.}
elements.sent-2411-033: Section \ref{sec:glossary contains a glossary of technical terms.}
elements.sent-2411-034: For future reference we provide some basic definitions here.
elements.sent-2411-035: A tree-decomposition of a graph $G$ consists of a pair $(T, X)$ where $T$ is a tree\footnote{Not a subtree of $G$; i.e., an abstract tree.}
elements.sent-2411-036: The {\bf width of the tree-decomposition is $\max_{t \in V(T) |X_t| - 1$.\end{DE Comment: the sets $X_t$ are referred to as the ``bags'' of the tree-decomposition.}}}
elements.sent-2411-037: The treewidth of a graph $G$ is the minimum width of any tree-decomposition of $G$.
elements.sent-2411-038: If we constrain the tree-decompositions to paths, the minimum width is termed the pathwidth.. \end{DE.}
elements.sent-2411-039: Here we describe a set of problems that can be rendered into the form QIB with low treewidth, thus obtaining polynomial-time approximation algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-040: There is a large literature on problems of the following form; see, e.g., \cite{Bienstock1996 , \cite{BertsimasRyan : \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\min \ x^T Q x \ - \ \mu^T x s.t. \ Ax \le b \label{eq:body} \ 0 \le x_j \le u_j z_j, \ z_j \in \{0,1\}, \ 1 \le j \le n \ \sum_{j = 1}^n z_j \ \le \ N. \label{eq:thecard}\end{equation} \end{subequations Here, $Q \succeq 0$ is a positive multiple of a covariance matrix, and $\mu$ is a vector.}}}}
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}\min \ x^T Q x \ - \ \mu^T x s.t. \ Ax \le b \label{eq:body} \ 0 \le x_j \le u_j z_j, \ z_j \in \{0,1\}, \ 1 \le j \le n \ \sum_{j = 1}^n z_j \ \le \ N. \label{eq:thecard}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-041: The matrix $A$, often, has few rows compared to $n$, though dense.
elements.sent-2411-042: In the textbook form of the problem, \eqref{eq:body just consists of the equation $\sum_j x_j = 1$.}
elements.sent-2411-044: It is known that this problem is NP-hard even under very restrictive conditions.
elements.sent-2411-045: If the number of rows in $A$ is part of the input the problem is strongly NP-hard.
elements.sent-2411-046: There is one more feature that is critical in real-world instances of this problem.
elements.sent-2411-047: Namely, the quadratic part of the objective is rendered as \begin{equation}\label{eq:lowrankplussparse} x^T Q x \ = \ x^T\tilde Q x \ + \sum_j d_j x^2_j\end{equation} Here $\tilde Q \succeq 0$ is of low rank and $d_j \ge 0$ for all $j$.
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation}\label{eq:lowrankplussparse} x^T Q x \ = \ x^T\tilde Q x \ + \sum_j d_j x^2_j\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-048: This is an example of a ``low rank plus sparse'' structure and we can take advantage of this feature to reduce to a case of problem QIB by diagonalizing the quadratic $x^T \tilde Q x$.
elements.sent-2411-049: This yields formulation \eqref{eq:portoptsparse , discussed immediately below.}

elements.subsection-2411-003: Convex quadratic optimization problems with banded matrices and indicator variables
elements.sent-2411-076: Here we consider a problem of the following form studied in \cite{Gomez2024 \begin{equation}\label{eq:gomez} h^* \ \doteq \ \min \quad d^T z \ + \ c^T x \ + \ x^T Q x s.t.\quad x \in \bR^n, \ z \in \{0,1\}^n, \ x_j =0 \ if \ z_j = 0, \ \forall j,\nonumber\end{equation} where $Q \succ 0$ is a banded matrix with bandwidth $\le k$, i.e., $Q_{ij = 0$ if $|i - j| > k$.}}
elements.formula-2411-005: \begin{equation}\label{eq:gomez} h^* \ \doteq \ \min \quad d^T z \ + \ c^T x \ + \ x^T Q x s.t.\quad x \in \bR^n, \ z \in \{0,1\}^n, \ x_j =0 \ if \ z_j = 0, \ \forall j,\nonumber\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-077: The very interesting algorithm in \cite{Gomez2024 , given $0 < \epsilon < 1$, computes a value $h$ with $|h - h^*| < \epsilon$, while performing at most \begin{equation}n \left(\frac{C \| d \|_{\infty}^2n}{\epsilon} \right)^{1 + \frac{k}{| \log \gamma|}}\end{equation} operations, where $\gamma = \frac{\sqrt{\text{cond (Q) - 1 {\sqrt{\text{cond (Q) + 1}$ (cond($Q$) is the ratio of the largest to the smallest eigenvalue of $Q$) and $C$ is a constant proportional to the largest entry of $Q$ and $(1 - \gamma^{1/k )^{-1}$ \footnote{The algorithm may compute square roots; however we expect that an approximate version with similar attributes applies under the bit model of computing.. .}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-078: To apply our framework, note that any vector $x$ (for example, $(1, \ldots, 1)^T$) is feasible and yields an upper bound $\hat U$ on the optimal objective value.
elements.sent-2411-079: Using this upper bound, and since $Q \succ 0$ we can compute (in polynomial time) an upper bound on the $|x_j|$.
elements.sent-2411-080: Finally, using the $LDL^T$ decomposition of $Q$ \cite{GolubVanLoan , scaling and shifting the $x$ variables, we can rewrite the problem as \begin{equation}\label{eq:gomez2} \min \quad d^T z \ + \ c^T x \ + \ \sum_{j = 1}^n d_j y_j^2\ \ + \ f^T y s.t.\quad Lx - y \ = \ b, \label{eq:Lrows} \quad x \in [0,1]^n, \ y \in [0,1]^n, \ z \in \{0,1\}^n, \ x_j =0 \ if \ z_j = 0, \ \forall j,\nonumber\end{equation} where $d \ge 0$, $b \in \bR^n$ and $f \in \bR^n$.}
elements.formula-2411-007: \begin{equation}\label{eq:gomez2} \min \quad d^T z \ + \ c^T x \ + \ \sum_{j = 1}^n d_j y_j^2\ \ + \ f^T y s.t.\quad Lx - y \ = \ b, \label{eq:Lrows} \quad x \in [0,1]^n, \ y \in [0,1]^n, \ z \in \{0,1\}^n, \ x_j =0 \ if \ z_j = 0, \ \forall j,\nonumber\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-081: The $L$ matrix is lower-triangular and also of bandwidth $\le k$, and, by scaling, we may assume that each row of this matrix has $1$-norm equal to $1$.
elements.sent-2411-082: Formulation \eqref{eq:gomez2 is an example of QIB with $n$ mixed-integer constraints (linear equations, all) and no combinatorial constraints, where the treewidth is at most $k - 1$.}
elements.sent-2411-083: Hence our algorithm, applied to this problem, has runtime $O( n(2/\epsilon)^{2k )$.}
elements.sent-2411-084: The algorithm yields a superoptimal solution with infeasibility (over constraints) \eqref{eq:Lrows at most $(n+1)\epsilon$.}
elements.sent-2411-085: The infeasibilities arise in constraints \eqref{eq:Lrows and thus, by adjusting the $y$ values, we obtain a feasible solution whose value exceeds the lower bound our algorithm yields by at most $O( \sum_j(d_j + |f_j|) (n \epsilon + n^2 \epsilon^2))$ .}

elements.sent-2411-094: In what follows, the set of mixed-integer constraints incident with a block $B_i$ will be denoted by $\cM_i$.
elements.sent-2411-095: A {\bf sketch for $B_i$ is given by a pair $( \delta, (k_r \, : \, r \in \cM_i))$, where $\delta \in \{0,1\}$, and $k_r \in \bZ$, $-\lceil \epsilon ^{-1 \rceil \, \le \, k_r \, \le \, \lceil \epsilon^{-1 \rceil - 1, \, \forall \, r \in \cM_i$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-096: The {\bf value of the sketch equals: \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\min x[i]^T Q^0_i x[i] \ + \ \sum_{j \in B_i} d^0_j x^2_j \ + \ \sum_{j \in B_i} c^0_j x \ + \ v^0_i z_i s.t. \ x[i]^T Q^r_i x[i] \, + \, \sum_{j \in B_i} d^r_j x^2_j \, + \, \sum_{j \in B_i} c^r_j x_j \, + \, v^r_i z_i \, \le \, \epsilon (k_r + 1), \ \forall \, r \in \cM_i \label{eq:mixedr} z_i \ = \ \delta, \label{eq:fixedzi} 0 \ \le \ x_j \le u_j , \ for \ j \in B_i , if $\delta = 0$, $x_j = 0$ for all $j \in B_i$. \label{eq:blocki}\end{equation} \end{subequations If the problem is feasible, we say that the sketch is feasible and that its {\bf certificate is given by an optimal solution vector.}}}}
elements.formula-2411-009: \begin{equation}\min x[i]^T Q^0_i x[i] \ + \ \sum_{j \in B_i} d^0_j x^2_j \ + \ \sum_{j \in B_i} c^0_j x \ + \ v^0_i z_i s.t. \ x[i]^T Q^r_i x[i] \, + \, \sum_{j \in B_i} d^r_j x^2_j \, + \, \sum_{j \in B_i} c^r_j x_j \, + \, v^r_i z_i \, \le \, \epsilon (k_r + 1), \ \forall \, r \in \cM_i \label{eq:mixedr} z_i \ = \ \delta, \label{eq:fixedzi} 0 \ \le \ x_j \le u_j , \ for \ j \in B_i , if $\delta = 0$, $x_j = 0$ for all $j \in B_i$. \label{eq:blocki}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-098: Note that \eqref{sketchr is a convex QCQP with $\le \omega + 1$ constraints.}
elements.sent-2411-099: For each mixed-integer constraint $r$, $\sum_{i \in B \epsilon (k^i_r + 1) \, \le \, b^r + \epsilon | \bB^r |$.}
elements.sent-2411-100: For each combinatorial constraint $r$, $\sum_{i \in B v^r_i \delta^i \, \le \, b^r$.}

elements.subsection-2411-005: Dynamic programming algorithm for minimum-value sketch ensemble problem
elements.sent-2411-110: In what follows we denote by $G$ the constraint-block intersection graph of an instance of problem \eqref{QuadBlockInd .}
elements.sent-2411-111: Without loss of generality $G$ is connected.
elements.sent-2411-112: We are given a tree-decomposition $(T, X)$ of $G$ of width $\omega$.
elements.sent-2411-113: For ease of notation, we refer to the vertices of $T$ as nodes.
elements.sent-2411-114: We view $T$ as rooted at an arbitrary non-leaf node.. {\bf Comment.}
elements.sent-2411-115: Each block $B_i$ corresponds to a clique $K_i$ of $G$ and thus (as is well-known) the set of nodes $t$ such that $B_i \subseteq X_t$ form a subtree of $T$.
elements.sent-2411-116: Namely, for each block $B_i$ there is a leaf $t(i)$ of $T$ such that $X_t = K_i$ and conversely, for each leaf $t$ we have that $X_t = K_i$ for some block $i$.
elements.sent-2411-117: Then expand $T$ (and the tree-decomposition) by adding an edge $\{ q, t \}$ where $q$ is a new vertex, and set $X_q = K_i$.
elements.sent-2411-118: Let us call this the leaf designated for block $B_i$ This proves the first part of (a).
elements.sent-2411-119: For the converse, if there is a leaf $t$ which is not a designated leaf, then removing $t$ from $T$ yields a (smaller) tree decomposition by the first part of (a).
elements.sent-2411-120: Then resetting $X_u \leftarrow X_u - \{r\}$ yields a smaller tree-decomposition of $G$ by (a).
elements.sent-2411-121: It is possible that two distinct blocks $B_i, \, B_j$ give rise to the same clique in $G$, i.e., $K_i = K_j$.
elements.sent-2411-122: However, since the blocks are distinct the corresponding set of variables are different.
elements.sent-2411-123: The corollary highlights that the leaves of $T$ partition the set of variables (i.e., into the blocks).
elements.sent-2411-124: Next we provide a tree-driven generalization for the computation of a minimum-value sketch ensemble that is suitable for dynamic programming.
elements.sent-2411-125: We will rely on the following notation, given a node $t \in T$:.
elements.sent-2411-126: We say that a {\bf block $B_i$ is {\bf contained in $T_t$ if $\{ q \in T \, : \, K_i \subseteq X_q \} \subseteq T_t$.}}
elements.sent-2411-127: Recall that $K_i$ is the clique induced by $B_i$ in $G$.
elements.sent-2411-128: We write \boldmath $\beta_t $\unboldmath $\doteq$ set of blocks contained in $T_t$.

elements.sent-2411-130: For every node $t$, \boldmath $\bM_t$ \unboldmath and \boldmath $\bC_t$ \unboldmath are the sets of mixed-integer and combinatorial constraints $r \in X_t$ (resp.).
elements.sent-2411-131: We will develop, below, a dynamic-programming algorithm for the minimum-value feasible sketch ensemble problem.
